text_clean: Una joya arquitectónica de la cultura Chachapoyas. Puesta en valor recientemente y conjuntamente con el teleférico de Kuelap, son un excelente ejemplo de cómo la inversión convierte unas ruinas de gran valor histórico pero a veces olvidadas y sólo conocidas por unos pocos investigadores, estudiantes y aventureros, en un espacio cultural activo y que atrae a turistas nacionales y extranjeros convirtiendola en un polo de desarrollo.

text_clean: En el Museo de Sitio Chan Chan, que está muy cerca del complejo arqueológico “Chan Chan”, se encuentran resguardadas y exhibidas ​muchas piezas arqueológicas de cerámica, piezas líticas y de metales, textiles y restos óseos provenientes del complejo arqueológico y sus alrededores. Una de las cosas más interesantes para apreciar en el museo es una enorme maqueta que recrea las posibles dimensiones de la ciudad de “Chan Chan”, fue una ciudad precolombina de adobe, construida cerca a la costa por los chimúes. También es la ciudad construida en adobe más grande de América​ y la segunda en el mundo.

text_clean: The ruins of Chan Chan are definitely worth a visit! Located between Trujillo and Huanchaco it is a quick trip here. I personally would recommend getting a tour guide as there is alot to learn from these 2000 year old ruins. However, it's also nice having a wander round as these ruins are so spectacular.

It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!

text_clean: Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures
label: neutro

text_clean: Excelente lugar para conocer en la ciudad de Tarapoto, la catarata es algo fría pero una vez dentro vale la pena llegar a ese punto. Durante el viaje se puede parar en un mirador de la cordillera que permite ver dos cascadas a lo lejos, una de ellas Ahuasiyacu. La persona que nos llevó fue el señor Carlos Arévalo, conocido como el charapo. El hace recorridos a diferentes puntos de la ciudad y es muy atento y cuidadoso con el servicio. Los precios son módicos, si cotizas con otros tours puede ser un poquito más caro, la diferencia es que vas solo y te tomas el tiempo que deseas, buena experiencia de viaje.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.

Plaques in the historic park only state the name of the location/exhibit in Quechua, without anything relevant interpretive narrative to aid appreciation of its historic and cultural significance. I didn't even see a map of the park near the ticket offices/entrances. So self guided visitors can only follow the arrows to finish the tour. You must buy a partial (combo) tourist ticket for 4 historical sites, size a ticket for Saqsaywamam alone isn't available. But at the end of the self-guided tour, there is no signs pointing you to the next attraction included on the ticket. We had to ask at the ticket office since we didn't have a taxi waiting to take us there.

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...
label: positivo

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: negativo

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).
label: positivo

text_clean: If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.

Highly recommended to go with a guide to understand the cultural and spiritual importance of the place.

text_clean: These ancient geoglyphs created by the pre incan civilizations (Paracas and Nazca) are a sight to behold! It's still shrouded in mystery. These elaborate depictions of different animals and insects like hummingbirds, monkeys and spiders still blows my mind. There's a variety in patterns and shapes. Some are simple lines going across the landscape, while others depict spirals, similar to crop circles. The scale of these geoglyphs are so huge that it's beauty can only be truly appreciated from the sky. If you're in Nazca I would recommend going to see them. The flight is not cheap but I think it's worth it. If you suffer from motion sickness, the flight over the nazca lines might surprise you. The plane has to tilt at a certain angle on either side so you can see the geoglyphs. At first it might seem like fun but after 20 minutes you might start feeling very sick. The flight is it's own experience. Try to snap a quick picture and face forward again. Apart from this, I still recommend doing this.

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: neutro

You have different routes that you can do and when one of the guides showed us what are the options and the prices we trusted him. Then he did shorter and we discovered that he made us pay more. He said that he did the "percurso medio" but he did the short one and made us pay 100 soles in total after 45 min. If we remember well his name was Sebastian. We felt like we were scammed. It is really pity because all the archeological sites in the sacred valley are really interesting as well as the Ollantaytambo but it is a continuous burgeon and there are no fixed guide prices. It is really pity!

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).
label: negativo

Aunque he visto otro bus circular por ese camino. En el camino hay algunas piletas con toboganes. También se puede comer tanto en ese camino, como en todo Moche. Se sirve mucho pato.

text_clean: If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.

text_clean: Fascinating archaeological site just outside Lima! Highly recommended to go with a guide to understand the cultural and spiritual importance of the place.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!

text_clean: Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures

I didn't even see a map of the park near the ticket offices/entrances. So self guided visitors can only follow the arrows to finish the tour. You must buy a partial (combo) tourist ticket for 4 historical sites, size a ticket for Saqsaywamam alone isn't available. But at the end of the self-guided tour, there is no signs pointing you to the next attraction included on the ticket. We had to ask at the ticket office since we didn't have a taxi waiting to take us there.

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...
label: neutro

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: positivo

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).
label: neutro

text_clean: Fascinating archaeological site just outside Lima! The museum is modern, clean and very well curated, offering a clear explanation of the history of Pachacamac and the different cultures that lived here — from the Lima and Wari to the Inca civilization. The ruins are vast and impressive, with temples, plazas and stunning views of the valley and the Pacific Ocean. Entry costs S/15 per person, and you can explore the site either by walking or by car. Highly recommended to go with a guide to understand the cultural and spiritual importance of the place.

text_clean: Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures

input_modelo: aspecto: limpieza resena: Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures

El recorrido moderadamente extenso de las diversas ruinas del Santuario se puede hacer a pie, en bicicleta o en movilidad propia. Lamentablemente algunos detalles importantes de las ruinas no se pueden ver directamente si no desde una distancia considerable. Y se entiende el acceso restringido porque es lamentable ver en algunas zonas graffitis, grabados y otros atentados hechos por personas sin escrúpulos ni cultura que han deteriorado severamente éste patrimonio histórico y cultural de la humanidad.

text_clean: Fascinating archaeological site just outside Lima! The museum is modern, clean and very well curated, offering a clear explanation of the history of Pachacamac and the different cultures that lived here — from the Lima and Wari to the Inca civilization. The ruins are vast and impressive, with temples, plazas and stunning views of the valley and the Pacific Ocean. Entry costs S/15 per person, and you can explore the site either by walking or by car. Highly recommended to go with a guide to understand the cultural and spiritual importance of the place.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

text_clean: Obviously it's an experience to be at Machupicchu, one of the best I have had alongside Egypt, Petra and Ajanta Ellora. Few musts: 1. Reach the bus station 1 hr before your entrance time 2. Must have a guide at the ruins and tell your guide that you want to know about the place and the guide is not hired to only click pictures 3. Book the longer trails, like 1 and 2. 4. If fit enough, do the Montana Machupicchu hike additionally. But it's 2 hrs hike, so be aware of physical requirements. Specially coming down will have more stress on your knees and thigh 5. No restrooms inside the site. There is one before entering 6. You can take water bottle and some snacks with you. So good idea to carry them in a bag, specially if you are doing the hike 7. Selfie sticks are not allowed. But you may sneak in a small selfie stick inside your bag 8. Walking sticks are generally not allowed. But probably they make consideration for people hiking the mountain. Check at entrance 9. After coming down from the mountains, tell the guards you want to take panoramic pictures of the ruins. They will guide you through trails which are otherwise closed to the middle platform 10. There are some friendly llamas at the ruin. Try to entyce them near with grass/leaves and take good pictures
label: positivo

text_clean: El parque en si es totalmente espectacular y merecedor de una visita. Para conocerlo hemos hecho el trekking de Santa Cruz, de cuatro dias y tres noches. Recomendable contratarlo directamente en Huaraz, despues de preguntar en varios sitios. Los precios varian enormemente. Pagamos 290 soles cuando en el mismo trekking habia gente que habia pagado tres veces mas. Ojo a los intermediarios! Finalmente todas las gentes aportan sus clientes para formar un grupo unico. El nivel de guiaje es malo o muy malo, desde un punto de vista europeo. Como ejemplo, el guia fue capaz de dividir un grupo de 7 personas en cuatro subgrupos, de los cuales tres estaban totalmente perdidos. Ausencia de briefing, indicaciones totalmente ambiguas y desorganizacion total. Si decides ir, te aconsejo llevar el track en tu movil (facilmente conseguible en wikiloc) o bien no separarte de guia sino tienes mucha experiencia. Por todo lo demas, el trekking es una maravilla, rodeado de montañas de cinco y seis mil metros en todo el trayecto. Ah! Incluye saco y esterilla. Las condiciones higienicas dejan que desear y la calidad tambien. Sino llevas tu propio saco pide, y paga, uno de pluma. Por la noche estaras bajo cero...

text_clean: El museo de sitio es sencillo pero claro y conciso con datos claves y diagramas informativos bien explicados. El recorrido moderadamente extenso de las diversas ruinas del Santuario se puede hacer a pie, en bicicleta o en movilidad propia. Lamentablemente algunos detalles importantes de las ruinas no se pueden ver directamente si no desde una distancia considerable. Y se entiende el acceso restringido porque es lamentable ver en algunas zonas graffitis, grabados y otros atentados hechos por personas sin escrúpulos ni cultura que han deteriorado severamente éste patrimonio histórico y cultural de la humanidad.

text_clean: If you are in the city of Custo for the first time like me, I recommend starting your acquaintance by visiting Saqsayhuaman, buy a ticket at the main entrance (take a taxi, it is a high ride uphill) and you have three different options: buy a ticket for 70 soles for one day and all three nearby attractions, or just visit Saqsayhuaman. It includes this place, plus a tunnel in the rocks and a statue of Christ. The last option is to buy a ticket for 10 days for all the places around and the city, salt baths, sacred Valley and costs 130 soles. Choose, and I will tell you about this place Saqsayhuaman, it is interesting that alpacas and llamas always graze peacefully and calmly on a green pasture there, you can take pictures with them. This site is for ceremonies and is very busy during the ceremony on August 1. Now there are few people, but on weekends buses of tourists arrive. Near the entrance there are many souvenirs and local products wool. Keep in mind that there are no food stores, stock up on water. It rains constantly, take a raincoat, walk a little into the rocks to the tunnel and to the statue uphill. But the exit is separate and through it you can run into the city. If you like the night city, then from there it is beautiful to look at the city and take pictures, and in the evening the statue of Christ is illuminated.

text_clean: El museo de sitio y santuario de Pachacamac está a unos 40 minutos fuera de la ciudad de Lima. Es un lugar bastante extenso para recorrerlo a pie o en tu propio auto. Aparte del museo del sitio donde se muestra los objetos encontrados, se puede recorrer una ruta de 5 a 6 km dentro del santuario para observar varias edificaciones, pirámides y centros ceremoniales de piedra tallada y adobe de la época preinca. Tiene estacionamiento, acceso con silla de ruedas, guia turístico, cafetería (muy buena y variada), baños limpios y cómodos. Si vas con niños o adultos mayores seria mejor ir en auto deberás caminar bastante. La mejor época sería en invierno o primavera, en verano por el clima desértico y la calor es bastante sofocante hacer el recorrido. Por mejorar esta todo el camino que es de tierra y disponer de alquiler de movilidades como cuatrimotos, patinetes eléctricos, bicicletas, etc. También es necesario habilitar caminos de acceso más cercanos, por las calles del santuario... en algunos casos sólo se ven de muy lejos y no permiten apreciar las edificaciones en todo su esplendor!
label: positivo

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: neutro

text_clean: The guards act irresponsibly and put the guests' health at risk by taking away their water. Attached is a photo of the entrance where this happend. We did Circuit 3 and got through the entrance of circuit 3/4 without any problems. When we entered again (through the entrance to the Mountain, which is also for Circuit 1+2) our backpack was suddenly too big. We had (for 2 people) 3l of water in a drinking system built into the backpack, 2 breads in a foldable can, sunscreen and 2 rain jackets. So nothing big, no plastic waste and our backpack fit in the small model box. We discussed in Spanish whether they really wanted to take away our water for the strenuous hike up the mountain in the midday heat, but they remained adamant. This irresponsible behavior inevitably leads to dehydration, which is dangerous since there is no way to buy more water. Otherwise the purchased ticket will be useless. I am horrified by the conditions there and how carelessly they provoke medical emergencies.
label: neutro

text_clean: Yes it’s an impressive location, but nothing more than that. The whole visit is a cash grab: - extortionate prices to get to the site (Inca or Peru rail, plus the bus to the top) - they charge to use the bathrooms, yet they look and smell disgusting - our tour in English was terrible (tour guide was an old lady called Nohemi): half the time she was just walking silently telling us to either take pictures or take in the views. The historic information she gave was very basic, we had to continuously ask questions and still we were getting ambiguous or contradictory answers. At the end she had the audacity to ask for a tip! If you want a history lesson, go elsewhere. If you are after a hyped picture, you’re in the right place.

text_clean: So disappointed to have made a 40 minute journey across town to see Huaca Pucilana after reading about the site in an official leaflet, only to be told that you need a prior appointment that wasn't advertised on the leaflet I had. Upon showing the leaflet to the attendant, I'd have thought they would have showed a little more consideration given the leaflet was one of their own. Unfortunately there are times when common sense is outweighed by someone wearing a uniform, and they'd much prefer to sit around twiddling their thumbs. A walk around the site enabled us to get an idea of the sheer scale of the place, and to witness a guide showing only 2 people around. Really busy. To see their llamas caged in to such a small area when there's so much space for them to roam is a complete mystery to me. Disgraceful mistreatment of animals.

Una decepción realmente. Lo único bueno fue la proyección de la historia del Perú. Vivo fuera hace 2 años traté de ir con mi familia y también había una feria y no pudimos hacerlo. text_clean: This is an amazing place! Incredible location in the exuberant ANDES . The tracks going to the point are unforgettable, that's the essence and uniqueness of those evergreen mountains. On the other hand stay the construction itself : very modest and elementary for the time ( Incas like all American tribes ) were underdeveloped and it is evident in all their monuments around the continent. Lack of any written documents make the stories sound so funny and absurd! Tour guides are so amateurish that make me LOL! Extremely overpriced but that's the sad story of the mass tourism industry all around the world now!

On the other hand stay the construction itself : very modest and elementary for the time ( Incas like all American tribes ) were underdeveloped and it is evident in all their monuments around the continent. Lack of any written documents make the stories sound so funny and absurd! Tour guides are so amateurish that make me LOL! Extremely overpriced but that's the sad story of the mass tourism industry all around the world now!

text_clean: La Plaza Mayor de Lima es simplemente impresionante. Un lugar lleno de historia, arquitectura y vida. Rodeada por la Catedral, el Palacio de Gobierno y edificios coloniales hermosos, caminar por aquí es sentir el corazón del Perú latiendo fuerte. La plaza está bien cuidada, limpia y con jardines preciosos que invitan a sentarse, observar y disfrutar el ambiente. Siempre hay movimiento: turistas, locales, artistas y ceremonias oficiales que le dan un aire especial. Ideal para fotos, para aprender historia y para conectarse con la esencia de Lima. Visita obligada si vienes a la ciudad, tanto de día como de noche.

text_clean: This is one of the spectacular places in the world to visit. It is just as fascinating as you can imagine. I have been wanting to go here since I was a child, and I was not disappointed. Tips for travel: - Buy your tickets in advance. This can be done online. I bought mine from a company on trip advisor which included a hike up Huayna Picchu. It was a little more pricey than buying direct from the Peru tourism website, but I had the confidence of good customer service with people on the ground who could act as a liaison to ensure that I got my tickets. Print them out and bring it with you. - **BRING YOUR PASSPORT** - I recommend taking Peru Rail’s Vistadome train to Aguas Calientes. - Bus tickets up the mountain can be purchased on the spot in Aguas Calientes for $25 round-trip. No need to buy in advance. Purchasing can take 10 minutes, depending on the line. Cash only. Ticket office is located one block from the bus pick up spot. - Bring a poncho, umbrellas are not allowed. - If you are not hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend two hours in the citadel. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend a total of four hours, and be sure to bring water and hiking poles. I cannot emphasize this enough. - The afternoon is less crowded. I would recommend going at that time if your trip schedule allows it. - Be sure to go up to the Sun Gate when you first enter. - All circuits through the citadel are one way. It would be helpful if you plan in advance on which circuit you would like to take. - Bring some snacks, bottles of water, and sunscreen. - There is a hotel, restaurant, baggage storage, and restrooms outside of the entrance. Keep this in mind as you plan. The restroom cost a small fee, so keep coins with you. - And finally, take lots of photos.
label: neutro

text_clean: Experiencia muy agradable Tomamos el tren desde Cusco, el cual tiene a mi gusto un precio alto, buenas vistas pero las 5 horas de duración se hacen un poco tediosas, los asientos son cómodos pero no incluye nada más, Una vez llegamos a aguas Calientes, el lugar es encantador, Existen cientos de restaurantes, pero les recomiendo los que están a la Orilla del Río, algo más caros, pero con un sabor q vale la pena, Los restaurantes internos de las calles tienen precios de 25 soles por persona, pero el sabor no es bueno, y eso que probamos varios, además la porción es pequeña, y a veces el refresco cuesta lo mismo q el plato y no va incluido. Es mejor pagar en los otros 40-60 soles por plato con un gran sabor En cuanto a hospedaje ahí en Aguas Calientes, donde te deja el tren para el día siguiente ir a Machu Picchu, hay bastantes, no veo razón para gastar demasiado, todos cerca y algunos de arriba en las calles son bonitos y acogedores Al día siguiente tomamos el bus hacia Machu Picchu, una barbaridad 24 dólares ida y regreso Pero sino quieres caminar es la única forma, aunque creo están quitando la concesión a la operadora de los buses. Debes comprarlo con tiempo La entrada a Machu Picchu debe ser comprada con tiempo, pq se agotan los horarios más cotizados y son varias rutas, 1. Asegúrate de q entres temprano, pq hay una sección que se cierra a las 10am y los q llegan luego no pueden pasar, 2. Asegúrate q tu recorrido es el q necesitas hacer, hay varios, y el q me gusta es el completo 3. No incluye la entrada a las montañas, Waynapicchu, y otras rutas, esa aventura cuesta 50 dólares más, En la fila muchos guías ofrecen sus servicios, la verdad vale la pena ya arriba, pero comienzan con precios muy altos de 60 soles por persona, debes tener en cuenta que conforme se les agotan las oportunidades bajan hasta 20-30 soles x persona, si saben bien de la ruta y de lo que hablan, vale la pena, En general todo tarda unas 3-4 horas Muy recomendado, en esta época Julio, mucho sol, muy caliente.

text_clean: You should be well prepared but also come with extra time and money if you really want to see Machu Picchu. If you buy tickets months in advance think carefully about the day, time and route since this can not be changed. No matter what you read basically 90% of the guid3d tour offers do NOT have the ticket included, you need to be 100% sure that your offer does or your visit will be a nightmare. Also: if booked far on advance make sure you do not visit in rainy season or your pictures will not show much of Machu Picchu. If you plan to be more flexible and buy the tickets when you are in Aguas Calientes... and the tickets can only be bought there, no matter what you read elsewhere, be prepared for a lot of queueing. We queued on first day to get a number for the queue the second day in the morning. There we git a number for the same day in the evening and there we could then actually buy the ticket for the next day, so the third day we were there. Since we booked the train only one way and waited to book return until we knew our day and time to see Machu Picchu we could not return on day three and had to book a third night and left o day four. We have seen people who left on day 4 WITHOUT having been able to see Machupicchu!!! So do not think you go there and leave the next day after successfully visiting Machupicchu. We thought if we are lucky this was possible, if not we need two nights...well, we were tought differently and many, many, many others too. Every day 5.600 people are able to see Machupicchu max. It is a crazy place and you pay a premium for basically everything. Accommodation is about twice the price as in Cusco as is for most souvenirs. I paid 120 soles for a alpaka poncho after bargaining from 150... in cusco the lady started for the EXACT same poncho at 90 and then asked what I would like to pay...so probably could have bought it for 80 or even less.
label: neutro

text_clean: Machu Picchu es maravilloso, llegué con mucha Niebla a las 6:30 am . La ciudad se veía muy mística, después de subir Wayna Picchu la ciudadela estaba despejada, se apreciaba todo. No hay mejor sensación que caminar por los restos arqueológicos incas y aprender cómo ellos vivían. Es increíble cómo hacían encajar las rocas a la perfección en el estilo inca imperial. Puedes visitar el templo del Sol, el templo al cóndor, la caseta del guardian, entre otros. Definitivamente no dan ganas de irse. Lo único malo es que el baño está fuera del parque, y si has comprado la entrada a Machu Picchu (sin Wayna) puedes salir solo una vez y no te dejan volver a entrar. Además te cobran por usar el baño, lo que encuentro un robo considerando que la entrada es bastante cara para un extranjero y ni siquiera incluye un guía.

text_clean: 500+ year old ruins of Incan imperial retreat. Stunning views, amazing history, good workout. Bring bug spray (mites/gnats will devour your legs). Walking sticks are helpful. Not wheelchair accessible, and extremely difficult for people with mobility challenges. Staff are friendly and helpful. One of the most photogenic places on earth. Visited on 1st Oct. Sunny at 9am, overcast at 10am, raining at 11am, and sunny again by noon. Wasn't too terribly crowded, but everyone moves at their own pace, so sometimes you pass someone, other times you get passed. Llamas at the top of Circuit 2... not aggressive, will let you pet them if you take your time. Food and toilets available at the trailhead. No hired guides necessary. text_clean: Machu Picchu: A Must-See Wonder, with a Few Tips Machu Picchu truly lives up to the hype – it's a place of breathtaking beauty and incredible history. Hiring a guide at the bus station was the best decision we made. Our guide was not only incredibly knowledgeable, weaving tales of the Incas into the very stones, but she was also wonderful with our children and a whiz at getting those perfect family photos. A word of advice for those not accustomed to high altitudes: spend a day or two acclimating in Cusco beforehand. The city offers a wealth of activities and attractions, so you won't be bored. Trust me, it makes a huge difference at Machu Picchu. Speaking of getting to Machu Picchu, the bus situation from Aguas Calientes can be a bit chaotic. Signage isn't great, and boarding can feel rushed. We traveled with Inca Rail, and while the train itself was comfortable, the waiting area was overcrowded and a bit messy. Overall: Machu Picchu is an unforgettable experience. Just be prepared for the altitude and a bit of a scramble getting there. Tips: Hire a guide: It's worth it for the knowledge, stories, and photo assistance. Acclimate: Spend time in Cusco if you're not used to high altitudes. Manage expectations: The bus situation and train station can be less than ideal. Make sure tu buy your ticket in advance.

text_clean: Este lugar del centro de Lima Perú es un lugar muy maravilloso, y donde se respira parte de la historia colonial del Perú, sus edificaciones y su bella decoración en todo su esplendor. me parece que la plaza de armas de Lima con el grna palacio presidencial y rodeada de tantos hermosos balcones son un punto indiscutible para una visita, ademas hay restaurantes y centros comerciales a los alrededores que pueden sacar de un apuro a cualquier viajero.. recomendado. En su arrededor la arquitectura es nuy espectacular plaza es digna de un birreinato, sencillamente fue una tarde de disfrutar todos los espacios que la rodean y disfrutar un rico almuerzo en uno de los restaurantes mas emblematicos de la ciudad ubicado diagonal a la casa de la literatura de Peru. Indicar que la plaza Mayor, es hermosa por su arquitectura colonial,se puede observar el cambio de guardia en la mañana..Es el corazón de Lima,es un espacio seguro para caminar y punto de partida para la caminata por el centro histórico.A su alrededor se encuentra el palacio de gobierno,la catedral de lima,la iglesia del sagrario,el palacio arzobispal,el palacio municipal y el club de la unión.SE encuentra circundada por Jirón Junín,Jirón de la unión,Jirón de Huallaga y Jirón Carabaya.Cerca se encuentran restaurantes de comida típica y el malecón artesanal. les aconsejo apartarse una mañanita o tarde para ir a la plaza. Si ya están en Lima háganse una escapadita. Es tradicional, linda pintoresca y bien cuidada. Me recomendaron andar con cuidado con los objetos personales por esa zona yo no tuve inconvenientes, pero nunca está demás decirlo.. Enfrente hay una iglesia, la municipalidad, y es la típica foto en la plaza de Lima.. Vale la pena ir.. En la cercanía hay locales comerciales, no del estilo de artículos regionales.
label: positivo

text_clean: This is one of the spectacular places in the world to visit. It is just as fascinating as you can imagine. I have been wanting to go here since I was a child, and I was not disappointed. Tips for travel: - Buy your tickets in advance. This can be done online. I bought mine from a company on trip advisor which included a hike up Huayna Picchu. It was a little more pricey than buying direct from the Peru tourism website, but I had the confidence of good customer service with people on the ground who could act as a liaison to ensure that I got my tickets. Print them out and bring it with you. - **BRING YOUR PASSPORT** - I recommend taking Peru Rail’s Vistadome train to Aguas Calientes. - Bus tickets up the mountain can be purchased on the spot in Aguas Calientes for $25 round-trip. No need to buy in advance. Purchasing can take 10 minutes, depending on the line. Cash only. Ticket office is located one block from the bus pick up spot. - Bring a poncho, umbrellas are not allowed. - If you are not hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend two hours in the citadel. If you are hiking Huayna Picchu expect to spend a total of four hours, and be sure to bring water and hiking poles. I cannot emphasize this enough. - The afternoon is less crowded. I would recommend going at that time if your trip schedule allows it. - Be sure to go up to the Sun Gate when you first enter. - All circuits through the citadel are one way. It would be helpful if you plan in advance on which circuit you would like to take. - Bring some snacks, bottles of water, and sunscreen. - There is a hotel, restaurant, baggage storage, and restrooms outside of the entrance. Keep this in mind as you plan. The restroom cost a small fee, so keep coins with you. - And finally, take lots of photos.
label: negativo

text_clean: Visitar el Monasterio de Santa Catalina fue, sin exagerar, una de las experiencias más fascinantes que he vivido en Perú. No solo por su belleza arquitectónica, sino por la atmósfera que se respira en cada rincón: una mezcla envolvente de historia, silencio, espiritualidad y arte. Desde el primer momento que crucé sus enormes puertas de piedra, sentí que estaba entrando a otra época. El monasterio es una ciudadela en sí misma, con sus propias calles, pequeñas plazas, fuentes, cocinas, lavanderías y celdas que, en su momento, fueron ocupadas por monjas de clausura. Caminando por los pasillos de piedra volcánica, se puede sentir el aislamiento deliberado del mundo exterior y la devoción con la que vivían allí. Los colores de las paredes son vibrantes: el rojo terracota, el azul profundo y el blanco encalado contrastan perfectamente con la luz intensa del cielo arequipeño. Cada rincón es fotogénico, pero más allá de eso, todo parece cuidadosamente pensado para fomentar la contemplación. A pesar de que ahora es un lugar abierto al público, el silencio sigue predominando, como si el monasterio aún guardara los secretos de las mujeres que vivieron y murieron ahí. Una de las cosas que más me impresionó fue la celda de Sor Ana de los Ángeles Monteagudo, una beata arequipeña cuya habitación permanece casi intacta. También me llamó la atención la sencillez de los objetos que usaban, contrastando con el lujo con el que muchas de estas monjas, provenientes de familias aristocráticas, llegaron al monasterio. La visita guiada (altamente recomendable) me permitió entender mejor la historia del lugar, que data del siglo XVI. El guía explicó con claridad cómo se fue expandiendo el monasterio, cómo cambiaron las reglas de clausura con los años, y cómo ahora una pequeña comunidad de monjas aún vive en una zona restringida del recinto, separada del área turística. En definitiva, el Monasterio de Santa Catalina no es solo un monumento histórico, es una cápsula del tiempo. Me fui con la sensación de haber conocido un pedazo profundo de la historia colonial del Perú, pero también con una extraña paz interior. Es un lugar que invita a caminar despacio, a observar, y a reflexionar. Una joya imperdible en el corazón de Arequipa.
label: negativo

text_clean: Todo peruano debe conocer este lugar!!! Es excelente y sobre todo barato... Si vienes desde Chachapoyas puedes tomar tours q cobran entre 90 a 110 soles x persona todo incluido, hasta almuerzos... Por cuenta propia tomas una combi a las 6am a 15 soles aprox hasta el distrito de Nuevo Tingo, luego caminas 15 minutos hasta el local de embarque de transporte para el ingreso hacia los TELEFÉRICOS donde compras tú entrada (20.40 soles precio, incluye ida y vuelta) IMPORTANTE.. NO PERDER LOS TICKETS xq si los pierdes, compras otros para el regreso al precio inicial... una vez pagado, esperas tú turno y ellos te llevan en bus hasta la zona elevada del cerro para subir por fin a los telefericos, el trayecto es de 20 minutos aprox, luego te hacerlas a la casa amarilla donde pagas tú entrada a 30 soles y te permiten subir el camino de 2 km q demoran unos 45 minutos... Ya en la cima vaz a la derecha hasta el ingreso a la fortaleza donde entregas la entrada a compraste a 30 soles... Una vez adentro, DISFRUTA DEL PASEO, debes calcular tus tiempos xq el ticket del teleférico te menciona la hora de regreso... Así q atento... Igual te dan tolerancia pero si hay mucha gente puede q se compliquen los tiempos pues la cola se hace grande al regreso. Pásenla chvrs causitas! Sí desean alguien que les guíe en el tour les dejo el número de contacto del guía que sale en la foto de chaleco beige
label: positivo

text_clean: Es un convento que te permiten revisar sin limitación la mayoría de los recintos donde vivían las religiosas de la ciudad... rico en historia además que aprendemos de la vida de Sor Ana de Los Angeles y disfrutamos de su exquisita repostería (hay una cafetería con venta de postres y bebidas, es obvio, precio un poco elevado que afuera del monasterio). No tiene estacionamiento. No apto para silla de ruedas. Hay servicios higiénicos pequeños. El ingreso es 45 soles (un poco caro según mi opinión)... en el caso de peruanos hay promoción el último domingo de cada mes es de 15 soles mostrando tu DNI. Se puede adquirir el boleto de forma online o presencial. Al ingreso, te ofrecen el servicio de guía por todo el monasterio. Eres libre de elegir o rechazar. Ahi mismo hay un código QR para descargar una app que te muestra información del monasterio y un mapa del local. Además que en cada habitación o calle existe una descripción en diferentes idiomas. Tiempo aproximado de estancia mínimo 1 hora si vas sólo a tomarte fotos... voy ingresando varias veces con amigos extranjeros que les gusta la historia y añadido las fotografias sin personas de fondo en las calles, es de 2 horas aproximadamente. Recomendado 😊
label: neutro

text_clean: Machu Picchu: A Must-See Wonder, with a Few Tips Machu Picchu truly lives up to the hype – it's a place of breathtaking beauty and incredible history. Hiring a guide at the bus station was the best decision we made. Our guide was not only incredibly knowledgeable, weaving tales of the Incas into the very stones, but she was also wonderful with our children and a whiz at getting those perfect family photos. A word of advice for those not accustomed to high altitudes: spend a day or two acclimating in Cusco beforehand. The city offers a wealth of activities and attractions, so you won't be bored. Trust me, it makes a huge difference at Machu Picchu. Speaking of getting to Machu Picchu, the bus situation from Aguas Calientes can be a bit chaotic. Signage isn't great, and boarding can feel rushed. We traveled with Inca Rail, and while the train itself was comfortable, the waiting area was overcrowded and a bit messy. Overall: Machu Picchu is an unforgettable experience. Just be prepared for the altitude and a bit of a scramble getting there. Tips: Hire a guide: It's worth it for the knowledge, stories, and photo assistance. Acclimate: Spend time in Cusco if you're not used to high altitudes. Manage expectations: The bus situation and train station can be less than ideal. Make sure tu buy your ticket in advance.
label: neutro

text_clean: El museo de la Catedral intimida con la carga de historia que vibra en sus retablos. text_clean: A very beautiful trekking area and unlike anywhere else in Peru. We spent two days trekking this area without a guide or tour, which is totally unnecessary in this canyon. The trails couldn’t be easier to follow and the whole area is perfect for fit hikers but beginner trekkers. Don’t be fooled though, the steep drop into the canyon and painful return climb to the rim are harder than most hikes, and reminiscent of those found in the Grand Canyon. We did the long classic route which descends to Llahuar and loops to San Juan before climbing back up to Cabanaconde. This is typically done over three days but we got to Llahuar by 10:00 on day one so competed the second days hike as well. That day was around 20+ kilometers and tough but rewarding. The next morning, we climbed from San Juan to the rim, which was quite a bit longer and tougher than expected. We had decided to skip San Galle which is on most trek itineraries as it is the most touristy of the villages in the canyon. We stayed at Colibri Lodge for our night inside, which was one of the nicer guesthouse we found in South America. No reservation, just got lucky with a walk in and they had availability for us. The only downside of trekking here are the ridiculously limited bus options. Unless you are coming from or going to Arequipa, there really aren’t any public buses available. There are a handful of tourist buses, but they all make lame stops at expensive restaurants, viewpoints, etc and cost $50-65 USD for Puno or Cusco. We didn’t want to go all the way back to Arequipa since we were headed to Cusco, and managed to find a public bus option to get there without backtracking. It included a few steps and a little adventure, but cost just 50 soles and was actually pretty easy. And let’s face it, you wouldn’t be trekking in Peru if you didn’t like adventure. If you want specific info on the trekking routes and distances, how to do this without a guide, or most importantly all of the transport options for accessing the canyon and then getting to places other than Arequipa, search our name, Career Break Adventures and Colca Canyon or something of that nature. Have fun and enjoy the trek!

text_clean: Es un lugar maravilloso y encantador. Hay que llegar temprano, para avanzar con calma. Sea paciente con el clima, porque es impredecible. Sea buen turista para tomarse las fotos y dejar que otros puedan tomar sus fotos. Los niños deben evitar correr, por accidentes y por respeto al lugar. También preste atención a la historia, porque se aprende mucho. A la salida hay que hacer la fila para tomar el transporte de regreso. No arroje basura.

text_clean: Todo peruano debe conocer este lugar!!! Es excelente y sobre todo barato... Si vienes desde Chachapoyas puedes tomar tours q cobran entre 90 a 110 soles x persona todo incluido, hasta almuerzos... Por cuenta propia tomas una combi a las 6am a 15 soles aprox hasta el distrito de Nuevo Tingo, luego caminas 15 minutos hasta el local de embarque de transporte para el ingreso hacia los TELEFÉRICOS donde compras tú entrada (20.40 soles precio, incluye ida y vuelta) IMPORTANTE.. NO PERDER LOS TICKETS xq si los pierdes, compras otros para el regreso al precio inicial... una vez pagado, esperas tú turno y ellos te llevan en bus hasta la zona elevada del cerro para subir por fin a los telefericos, el trayecto es de 20 minutos aprox, luego te hacerlas a la casa amarilla donde pagas tú entrada a 30 soles y te permiten subir el camino de 2 km q demoran unos 45 minutos... Ya en la cima vaz a la derecha hasta el ingreso a la fortaleza donde entregas la entrada a compraste a 30 soles... Una vez adentro, DISFRUTA DEL PASEO, debes calcular tus tiempos xq el ticket del teleférico te menciona la hora de regreso... Así q atento... Igual te dan tolerancia pero si hay mucha gente puede q se compliquen los tiempos pues la cola se hace grande al regreso. Pásenla chvrs causitas! Sí desean alguien que les guíe en el tour les dejo el número de contacto del guía que sale en la foto de chaleco beige
label: neutro

text_clean: Visite la Plaza Mayor luego de varios años, en mi opinión una de las mejores del Perú, estuve un domingo donde no hay acceso vehicular, mucha gente entre turistas nacionales e internacionales disfrutan de los desfiles que se desarrollan en los alrededores, hay seguridad y puedes disfrutar los espacios, tomarte fotos, hay muchos lugares para comer. A los alrededores hay playas de estacionamiento y cobran entre 4 a 6 soles la hora.

Extremely overpriced but that's the sad story of the mass tourism industry all around the world now!

text_clean: La ciudadela de Kuélap es otra de nuestras maravillas que tiene nuestro Perú. Pará llegar es a través de los teleféricos. El costo es de S/. 10.00 soles por persona, tienes 2 maneras de llegar a caballo con un guía el precio es de S/. 30 soles ida y vuelta hay una caballeriza para llegar y abordar el retorno, el tramo es de aproximadamente 20 minutos, también se puede ir a pie pero demoraría 40 a 50 minutos. Al llegar a la ciudadela hay un guía que los lleva a recorrer las instalaciones y va explicando cómo nuestros antepasados Chachapoyas vivían en tan hermosa ciudadela. Hay centros para comprar recuerdos.
label: neutro
